Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict study routine. I'd usually just study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Being a hands-on learner, I found it hard to sit and read for hours, so I'd do stuff in short bursts between my extracurricular activities. Sometimes I'd go a few days without touching anything academic, then cram a bunch in one afternoon.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. If there was a deadline coming up, I'd suddenly become super focused for a day or two. But when things were calm, I'd drift and focus on other stuff like my activities outside of school. I never really locked into a consistent weekly schedule.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due or I got a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, but I wouldn't always do much once I was there. If a friend mentioned something about the course, that would also remind me to check in.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I naturally went to every day. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I was logging in more regularly because we had back-to-back assignments, but once that pressure lifted, I went back to only checking when necessary. It just never stuck as a habit for me.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty useful because I could watch them and see things being demonstrated, which works better for how I learn. I'd watch them at 1.5x speed sometimes and then try to apply what I saw. Discussion posts were okay too because seeing other people's questions helped me realize what I was missing.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and extra resources. PDFs and long articles just aren't my thing—I'd open them, scroll a bit, then close them. I learn better by doing, so unless something required me to actually practice or apply knowledge, I probably skipped it or barely skimmed it.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd put in the effort. Activities that let me actually do something—like practice problems or interactive stuff—felt worth my time. But if it was just reading for the sake of reading with no clear payoff, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like it didn't matter as much. I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. Required activities had a deadline and consequences, so I'd actually do them. Optional ones just sat there, and with limited time and energy, they always got pushed aside.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download slides or videos and go through them on my phone when I was out or during breaks from my extracurriculars. I also used YouTube a lot to find videos that explained concepts in more visual or practical ways. None of that would show up in the LMS at all.
Q10: There were times I'd study with friends in person, working through problems together or quizzing each other. We'd use printed notes or stuff on our phones. I also liked to just think through concepts while doing other activities, like working out. That kind of learning doesn't leave any digital trace.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never open it in the LMS again. I'd have it saved on my laptop or phone and refer back to it multiple times. The system would only see that one download, but I might have reviewed it three or four times offline.
Q12: Around midterms when everything piled up at once, I felt pretty disconnected from the course. I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars too, so the course took a backseat. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just had too many things competing for my attention and something had to give.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or stressed from other stuff, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of zone out. I might click around without really absorbing anything. If I had responsibilities outside of school, those usually won because they felt more immediate or important in the moment.
Q14: Yeah, there were plenty of times I'd open a video or reading and my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd technically be 'studying' but not actually taking anything in. Sometimes I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes of a video and couldn't tell you a single thing it covered.
Q15: I put off assignments a lot, even when I knew they mattered. It's like I'd tell myself I work better under pressure, but really I just didn't want to start. The longer I waited, the more stressful it got, but I still kept doing it. It's a bad habit I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd be pretty inactive until a deadline was close, then suddenly I'd be all over the LMS, watching videos, downloading stuff, trying to finish assignments. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged with the course much at all.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. If I got good feedback, it felt nice but didn't really change what I did. If I got critical feedback, I'd feel a bit bummed but usually moved on without making major changes. I know I should use feedback more, but I just didn't prioritize it.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which was good and bad. Good because I could work at my own pace, but bad because I need more external pressure to stay on track. If the course had more regular check-ins or smaller deadlines, I probably would have engaged more consistently.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once made it hard to keep track of everything. When the course had clear, simple instructions and shorter tasks, I was more likely to engage. But when things were vague or required a lot of reading, I'd drift away and do the bare minimum.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to figure out what's expected without actually doing the work. Or they could be like me—clicking around, watching videos, but not following through on assignments because something else comes up or they just lose motivation.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. I don't log in a ton, but I study in other ways and manage to pass. I think if you know how to learn efficiently outside the system, you don't need to be on the LMS constantly. My exam scores were decent even though my online activity was low.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, group work, and thinking you do away from the screen. It's like measuring effort by counting steps instead of seeing the whole journey.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort, even when it's hard. It's not just about showing up or clicking through stuff—it's about being mentally present. For me, I'm engaged when I'm doing something hands-on that makes the material click.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm actually pretty motivated when something interests me, or that I learn best through doing rather than reading. It wouldn't capture my conversations with classmates or the times I figured something out while away from my computer. There's a lot happening that data just can't see.
